rationale: Title X conveys the specific clinical strategies (drug-switching as first-line, LAAC as last resort), the sample size (196 physicians), and the geographic scope (European), giving readers a complete picture of the study's design and findings at a glance. This level of specificity is well-aligned with Europace's empirical survey tradition. Title Y is grammatically clean but sacrifices nearly all informative content — 'contemporary practice' is generic and the expanded acronym 'European Heart Rhythm Association' adds length without adding clinical substance beyond what 'European' already implies.
loser_weakness: Title Y is too vague: 'contemporary practice' conveys no finding or specific content, the EHRA expansion adds wordiness without informational gain, and the absence of sample size or any clinical detail makes the title indistinguishable from dozens of other practice surveys.
